summary: Cantrill argues that USENIX has become disconnected from its mission of supporting research with a 'practical bias,' with the General Session dominated by academic papers that solve problems too small to be relevant to industry practitioners. Drawing a parallel to mechanical engineering, he suggests that systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require resources beyond what a single graduate student can provide, yet unlike other engineering fields, USENIX hasn't adapted by integrating industry practitioners into its program committees. He proposes eliminating the FREENIX track and folding industry voices into the main program to prevent the conference from becoming irrelevant.
key_points: DTrace paper was the only paper at USENIX '04 to come exclusively from industry
Academic systems research solves problems 'sufficiently small to be solvable by a single graduate student'
Engineering disciplines like automotive stopped academic programs when contributing to state-of-the-art became too expensive for academia alone
USENIX '05 Program Committee has inverse ratio of academia to industry compared to traditional engineering conferences
FREENIX track risks becoming a 'dumping ground' for practically-biased work, separating academics from practitioners
money_quotes: The content of the General Session was thus academic in the strictest sense: it consisted of papers written by graduate students, solving problems in systems sufficiently small to be solvable by a single graduate student working for a reasonably short period of time.
The problem is that many of these systems – to me at least – are so small as to not be terribly relevant.
These programs were stopped because the economics no longer made sense: it became prohibitively expensive to meaningfully contribute to the state-of-the-art.
The disciplines were mature enough that there was an established industry that understood that research begat differentiated product, and differentiated product begat profit.

Having the General Session become strictly academic and leaving the FREENIX track to become strictly industrial effectively separates the academics from the practitioners. And this, in my opinion, is exactly what we don't need.
How do we prevent USENIX from becoming irrelevant?
key_insight: Systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require industry-scale resources, and academic conferences must integrate practitioners or risk irrelevance.
